The meal (buttered bagel, fried egg, sausage) is a clear fried/fatty/meat meal, which ASA stratifies into the 8-or-more-hour fasting category. The stratification in the explanation is accurate and current: clear liquids 2h, breast milk 4h, infant formula/nonhuman milk/light meal 6h, fried/fatty/meat 8h+. Verified against independent authoritative sources (OpenAnesthesia, GuidelineCentral summaries of the ASA Task Force guideline). All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are correct (2h=clear liquids; 4h=breast milk; 6h=light meal/toast+clear liquids, explicitly not fatty/fried; "no fasting"=clearly wrong). The explanation's timing math is correct (9 AM meal to 1 PM start = 4h, so case should be delayed). Minor non-defect nuance: ASA phrases the 8h figure as a consideration ("additional fasting time may be needed") rather than an absolute mandate, but the stem asks for the "minimum recommended fasting interval," for which 8h+ is the standard board answer.

REFERENCE: The cited URL (https://www.asahq.org/~/media/sites/asahq/files/public/resources/standards-guidelines/practice-guidelines-for-preoperative-fasting.pdf) resolves to HTTP 200 and serves a genuine 178 KB PDF of the ASA preoperative fasting guideline. WebFetch returned 403 only due to ASA anti-bot blocking; a real browser user-agent (curl) downloads the valid PDF at the exact cited path. The path segment and filename are exactly on-topic, and the reference_title matches the ASA Task Force guideline. Citation is genuine and on-topic.

stem: During a preoperative evaluation for an elective knee arthroscopy, a 19-year-old man mentions that his maternal uncle died unexpectedly during a routine appendectomy years ago after 'his temperature shot up and his muscles went rigid' under anesthesia. The patient himself is healthy, takes no medications, and has never had surgery. There is no known genetic testing in the family. Which of the following is the most appropriate anesthetic plan for this patient?

choices.text: Proceed with a standard volatile-based general anesthetic and have dantrolene immediately available
choices.rationale: Having dantrolene available does not justify exposing a patient with a suggestive family history to triggering volatile agents when a non-triggering technique is feasible.

choices.text: Administer succinylcholine for rapid-sequence induction but avoid all volatile agents
choices.rationale: Succinylcholine is itself a potent malignant hyperthermia trigger and must be avoided, not just the volatile agents.

choices.text: Use a non-triggering technique (total intravenous anesthesia and a flushed/prepared machine) and avoid all volatile agents and succinylcholine
choices.rationale: Until proven otherwise, a patient with a first-degree-related family history suggestive of malignant hyperthermia should be treated as susceptible, so a non-triggering anesthetic that avoids all volatile agents and succinylcholine is correct.

choices.text: Cancel the case and require a muscle biopsy contracture test before any anesthetic can be given
choices.rationale: An elective case can safely proceed with a non-triggering technique, and demanding contracture testing before surgery is unnecessary and impractical for routine care.

choices.text: Give a prophylactic loading dose of intravenous dantrolene before induction, then proceed with a volatile anesthetic
choices.rationale: Routine prophylactic dantrolene is no longer recommended and exposing a susceptible patient to volatile triggers is unsafe; avoidance of triggers is the standard.

explanation: Malignant hyperthermia is an autosomal dominant pharmacogenetic disorder, most commonly involving RYR1 variants, triggered by volatile halogenated anesthetics and succinylcholine. A family history suggestive of an MH event places this patient in the susceptible category until formal testing (contracture testing or genetic analysis) proves otherwise. The safe approach for an elective case is a non-triggering anesthetic: total intravenous anesthesia, avoidance of all volatile agents and succinylcholine, a vapor-free or thoroughly flushed/prepared machine, and dantrolene readily available. Routine prophylactic dantrolene is no longer recommended.
subtopic: Inherited disease screening in preoperative evaluation (malignant hyperthermia susceptibility)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Keyed answer (index 2 — non-triggering TIVA technique, avoid all volatiles and succinylcholine, vapor-free/flushed machine, dantrolene available) is the single best answer for a patient with a family history suggestive of MH; until formal testing proves otherwise, treat as susceptible. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A and E both deliberately expose a susceptible patient to triggering volatiles (E also relies on prophylactic dantrolene, which is no longer recommended); B proposes succinylcholine, itself a potent MH trigger; D unnecessarily cancels/demands contracture biopsy when non-triggering anesthesia is safe. Genetics (autosomal dominant, RYR1) and trigger list are correct and current. Minor non-disqualifying wording nit: the rationale for choice C calls the maternal uncle 'first-degree-related,' but an uncle is a third-degree relative — this imprecision does not change the answer (a clearly suggestive event in any relative warrants susceptible precautions). REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430828/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Malignant Hyperthermia' article, directly on-topic. It confirms halogenated agents + succinylcholine as triggers, RYR1/autosomal dominant genetics, non-triggering/IV anesthetic for susceptible patients, dantrolene treatment, and explicitly states prophylactic pretreatment 'should not be done' — fully supporting the explanation. Keep.

stem: A 34-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury at the T4 level sustained 8 months ago presents for cystoscopy and bladder stone removal under monitored anesthesia care with sedation. Shortly after the surgeon distends the bladder with irrigation fluid, the patient develops a pounding headache, facial flushing, and profuse sweating above the nipple line. His heart rate falls from 78 to 46 beats/min and his blood pressure rises from 118/74 to 212/118 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.text: Ask the surgeon to stop the procedure and drain the bladder, sit the patient upright, and loosen constrictive items
choices.rationale: Correct: removing the noxious stimulus below the injury level and using gravity to lower blood pressure are the first-line, most effective interventions for autonomic dysreflexia.

choices.text: Administer intravenous atropine to treat the bradycardia before addressing the blood pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the bradycardia is a reflex baroreceptor-mediated response to severe hypertension and resolves once the inciting stimulus and the hypertension are corrected, not by treating heart rate first.

choices.text: Place the patient in Trendelenburg position to improve cerebral perfusion
choices.rationale: Incorrect: head-down positioning worsens the dangerous hypertension by raising central blood volume and cerebral pressure, the opposite of what is needed.

choices.text: Give a rapid intravenous fluid bolus and phenylephrine for presumed hypovolemic instability
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the patient is acutely hypertensive from unopposed sympathetic outflow, so volume loading and a vasopressor would dangerously exacerbate the crisis.

choices.text: Deepen sedation with a propofol infusion and continue the procedure once vitals stabilize
choices.rationale: Incorrect: deepening sedation does not remove the bladder distension driving the reflex, and continuing the stimulus risks hypertensive stroke, seizure, or arrhythmia.

explanation: Autonomic dysreflexia occurs in patients with spinal cord injury at or above T6, where a noxious stimulus below the lesion (most often bladder distension or a urologic source) triggers massive unopposed sympathetic outflow and severe hypertension, with reflex bradycardia and flushing/sweating above the lesion. The cornerstone of management is immediate removal of the inciting stimulus and upright positioning to lower blood pressure; pharmacologic vasodilators (e.g., nitroprusside, nitroglycerin, or nifedipine) are reserved for hypertension that persists after these maneuvers. Treating the reflex bradycardia or giving vasopressors misreads the underlying sympathetic crisis.
subtopic: Autonomic dysreflexia in spinal cord injury

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Textbook autonomic dysreflexia vignette that is internally consistent and unambiguous. T4 complete SCI (at/above T6), 8 months post-injury (past spinal shock), bladder distension trigger, hypertensive surge (118/74 -> 212/118) with reflex bradycardia (78 -> 46) and flushing/sweating above the lesion ("above the nipple line" correctly maps to the T4 dermatome). The keyed answer (index 0) — stop/drain the inciting stimulus, sit upright, loosen constrictive items — is the universally accepted first-line management and is clearly the single best choice. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and several are dangerous: atropine treats a benign reflex bradycardia while ignoring the crisis; Trendelenburg worsens hypertension; fluids+phenylephrine misread the surge as hypovolemia and would exacerbate it; deepening sedation fails to remove the stimulus. The explanation correctly reserves vasodilators (nitroprusside, nitroglycerin, nifedipine) for persistent hypertension. REFERENCE: StatPearls "Autonomic Dysreflexia" (NBK482434) resolves and is precisely on-topic — confirms T6-or-above level, urologic/bladder triggers (~85%), upright positioning + removing constrictive items as initial steps, and the reflex bradycardia mechanism. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 58-year-old woman is undergoing suboccipital craniotomy for resection of an acoustic neuroma in the sitting position under general anesthesia with nitrous oxide, oxygen, and sevoflurane. About 90 minutes into the case, the precordial Doppler tone suddenly changes to a harsh mill-wheel pattern, end-tidal CO2 falls abruptly from 34 to 19 mmHg, and the systolic blood pressure drops from 110 to 72 mmHg. Which of the following best combines the correct first interventions?

choices.text: Increase the nitrous oxide concentration to maintain anesthetic depth and continue the resection
choices.rationale: Incorrect: nitrous oxide diffuses into the air bubble and enlarges the embolus, so it must be discontinued immediately, not increased.

choices.text: Notify the surgeon to flood the field with saline, discontinue nitrous oxide, and administer 100% oxygen
choices.rationale: Correct: alerting the surgeon to occlude the air entry point with irrigation and bone wax, stopping nitrous oxide to prevent bubble expansion, and giving 100% oxygen are the appropriate immediate steps.

choices.text: Lower the head of the operating table below heart level and reduce intravenous fluids to limit cerebral edema
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although lowering the surgical site reduces further air entrainment, restricting fluids is wrong because volume expansion raises venous pressure and helps oppose air entry.

choices.text: Apply positive end-expiratory pressure of 15 cmH2O as the single most effective definitive treatment
choices.rationale: Incorrect: high PEEP is not reliably protective and can paradoxically promote paradoxical air embolism through a patent foramen ovale, so it is not the recommended first move.

choices.text: Immediately give intravenous epinephrine and begin chest compressions for presumed cardiac arrest
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the patient has a perfusing rhythm with hypotension, so compressions are not yet indicated and the priority is stopping air entrainment and supporting circulation.

explanation: Venous air embolism is a feared complication of sitting-position posterior fossa surgery because the operative site is above the heart and non-collapsible venous sinuses can entrain air, producing a sudden fall in end-tidal CO2, the classic mill-wheel Doppler murmur, and hemodynamic collapse. Immediate management is to notify the surgeon to flood/occlude the field, discontinue nitrous oxide (which expands the air bubble), deliver 100% oxygen, and support the circulation with fluids and vasopressors; aspiration of air from a right-atrial catheter may be attempted but should not delay hemodynamic support. Lowering the surgical site and jugular compression can reduce further entrainment.
subtopic: Venous air embolism in the sitting position (neuroanesthesia positioning)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Stem is a textbook VAE presentation in sitting-position posterior fossa surgery (mill-wheel Doppler tone, abrupt ETCO2 fall 34→19, hypotension) with a perfusing rhythm. Keyed answer B (notify surgeon to flood/occlude field, discontinue nitrous oxide, give 100% O2) is the single best first-intervention bundle. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: (A) increasing N2O expands the bubble — wrong by inversion; (C) fluid restriction is wrong because volume/elevated venous pressure opposes air entry (rationale correctly targets this); (D) PEEP 15 is not reliably protective and risks paradoxical embolism via PFO; (E) chest compressions not indicated with a perfusing rhythm. Rationales and the explanation (mention of right-atrial catheter aspiration, lowering surgical site, jugular compression as adjuncts) are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/venous-air-embolism/ resolved successfully and is directly on-topic — it explicitly states the hallmark of treatment is preventing further air entry by notifying the surgeon to flood the field with saline / apply bone wax, administering 100% oxygen (discontinue nitrous oxide if applicable), and giving vasopressors, and it covers precordial Doppler, mill-wheel murmur, ETCO2 fall, and sitting-position neurosurgery risk. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 27-year-old man is scheduled for an urgent tendon transfer 3 weeks after a traumatic brachial plexus avulsion injury that left his right arm densely paralyzed. He has no other medical history and laboratory values are normal. During induction, the anesthesiologist assistant is asked whether succinylcholine is an appropriate choice to facilitate rapid intubation. Which statement best explains the primary concern?

choices.text: Succinylcholine is safe here because receptor changes after denervation take at least 6 months to develop
choices.rationale: Incorrect: extrajunctional receptor upregulation begins within a few days of denervation, well before this patient's three-week point, so the drug is not safe to assume.

choices.text: Succinylcholine may cause exaggerated, potentially fatal hyperkalemia due to upregulation of extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors
choices.rationale: Correct: denervation injury triggers proliferation of extrajunctional and immature/alpha-7 acetylcholine receptors, so succinylcholine depolarizes a vastly expanded receptor pool and releases a large potassium load.

choices.text: Succinylcholine will be ineffective because denervated muscle no longer responds to depolarizing agents
choices.rationale: Incorrect: denervated muscle remains responsive and is in fact hypersensitive due to receptor upregulation, so the concern is excessive response, not lack of effect.

choices.text: Succinylcholine is contraindicated mainly because it predictably triggers malignant hyperthermia in nerve-injury patients
choices.rationale: Incorrect: peripheral nerve injury does not predispose to malignant hyperthermia, which is a distinct pharmacogenetic disorder of the ryanodine receptor.

choices.text: Succinylcholine should be avoided because denervation prolongs plasma cholinesterase activity and dramatically extends block duration
choices.rationale: Incorrect: denervation does not increase cholinesterase activity or meaningfully prolong succinylcholine duration; the danger is hyperkalemia, not an extended block.

explanation: After upper or lower motor neuron denervation, burns, prolonged immobilization, or severe muscle injury, skeletal muscle upregulates extrajunctional and immature (alpha-7) acetylcholine receptors across the membrane. Succinylcholine depolarizes this greatly expanded receptor population, producing massive efflux of intracellular potassium that can cause life-threatening hyperkalemia, arrhythmias, and cardiac arrest. The vulnerable window typically opens within a few days of injury and persists for months, so a nondepolarizing agent should be chosen for this patient rather than succinylcholine.
subtopic: Succinylcholine-induced hyperkalemia in denervation/neuromuscular injury
reference_title: Suxamethonium-Induced Hyperkalemia: A Short Review of Causes and Recommendations for Clinical Applications (PMC)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 1) is the single best answer. Brachial plexus avulsion is a lower-motor-neuron denervation injury, the textbook cause of succinylcholine-induced hyperkalemia from upregulation of extrajunctional/immature (alpha-7) nAChRs. At 3 weeks post-injury the patient is squarely within the well-established danger window. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and accurately refuted: (A) the claim that receptor changes take 6 months is false—upregulation begins within days, consistent with both the cited source (detectable K+ rise within 6h, critical by 72h) and Miller's/Stoelting; (B) denervated muscle is hypersensitive, not unresponsive; (C) peripheral nerve injury does not predispose to MH (distinct RYR1 disorder); (D) denervation does not increase plasma cholinesterase or prolong block—the hazard is hyperkalemia. Explanation is accurate and current. Stem is unambiguous. REFERENCE: reference_url resolves (301 redirect to current PMC host pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov) and the article 'Suxamethonium-Induced Hyperkalemia: A Short Review of Causes and Recommendations for Clinical Applications' is directly on-topic—covers the mechanism (AChR proliferation, immature receptors, larger K+ efflux), denervation as a risk factor, and the post-denervation time course. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 39 weeks gestation receives a labor epidural that has provided good analgesia for 4 hours. She is now taken for cesarean delivery for arrest of dilation. After negative aspiration, the anesthesiologist administers 18 mL of 2% lidocaine with epinephrine through the existing epidural catheter in divided doses. Within 4 minutes she reports difficulty breathing and tingling in her hands, then becomes unable to speak and loses consciousness. Blood pressure falls to 68/34 mm Hg, heart rate is 44/min, and she becomes apneic. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate priority?

choices.text: Place the patient in steep reverse Trendelenburg to limit cephalad spread of local anesthetic
choices.rationale: Once a large dose is already in the subarachnoid space, repositioning does not meaningfully reverse established high block and delays the airway and circulatory rescue that the patient urgently needs.

choices.text: Administer 20% intravenous lipid emulsion for suspected local anesthetic systemic toxicity
choices.rationale: The picture of progressive ascending sensory/motor block, apnea, and bradycardic hypotension after a neuraxial dose is a total spinal from unrecognized intrathecal catheter migration, not the seizures/arrhythmias of systemic toxicity, so lipid emulsion is not the immediate priority.

choices.text: Secure the airway with intubation and ventilation while supporting circulation with a vasopressor such as epinephrine and aggressive fluids
choices.rationale: Apnea plus profound hypotension and bradycardia from a total spinal is managed by immediate ventilation/intubation and circulatory support (vasopressor, fluids, left uterine displacement), which directly treats the life-threatening physiology.

choices.text: Administer a 500 mL crystalloid bolus and observe, anticipating spontaneous recovery as the block recedes
choices.rationale: A fluid bolus alone is inadequate for an apneic patient with bradycardic shock, and passive observation risks maternal and fetal hypoxic injury during the time the block takes to regress.

choices.text: Give atropine 0.5 mg IV as definitive treatment and continue spontaneous ventilation
choices.rationale: Atropine may help the bradycardia but does nothing for the apnea or the hypotension driven by extensive sympathectomy, so it cannot be the sole or definitive intervention.

explanation: Total (high) spinal anesthesia results from an unexpectedly large dose of local anesthetic reaching the subarachnoid space—classically when an epidural catheter has migrated intrathecally and a surgical epidural dose is given. The hallmark is rapid ascending block producing hypotension, bradycardia (high thoracic cardioaccelerator blockade), dyspnea, inability to phonate, and apnea, sometimes with loss of consciousness. Management is supportive and immediate: secure the airway and ventilate (intubate the apneic patient), support the circulation with vasopressors (epinephrine for combined bradycardia and hypotension), give fluids, maintain left uterine displacement, and prepare for emergent delivery.
subtopic: Neuraxial complications — total/high spinal after epidural top-up

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The vignette (working epidural topped up with a large surgical dose after negative aspiration → rapid ascending block, dyspnea, inability to phonate, LOC, apnea, plus bradycardia 44 and hypotension 68/34 from high cardioaccelerator/sympathetic blockade) is the textbook presentation of a total/high spinal from unrecognized intrathecal catheter migration. Keyed answer (index 2: secure airway/intubate, ventilate, support circulation with vasopressor such as epinephrine + fluids + LUD) is the correct immediate ABC priority and is guideline-concordant. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: reverse Trendelenburg (0) does not reverse an established high block and delays rescue; lipid emulsion (1) is the main trap but the picture is total spinal not LAST (no seizures/tachyarrhythmia, and time course/ascending block fit neuraxial spread) — and even in LAST, airway/ventilation and circulatory support remain the immediate priority over lipid; fluid bolus + observe (3) is inadequate for an apneic patient in bradycardic shock; atropine as 'definitive' with 'continue spontaneous ventilation' (4) ignores the apnea and the sympathectomy-driven hypotension and is internally contradictory (patient is apneic). Explanation accurately describes the pathophysiology and management. REFERENCE: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537299/ resolves to StatPearls 'Spinal Anesthesia,' which is on-topic — it covers neuraxial technique, explicitly names total/high spinal as 'the most feared complication,' and states ventilation/oxygenation/circulatory support must be in place. Title and content match the citation. No clinical or reference problem; keep.

stem: A 33-year-old G1P0 woman at 35 weeks gestation is admitted with severe preeclampsia (BP 172/112 mm Hg, proteinuria, headache). She is started on an intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion for seizure prophylaxis. Three hours later, before a planned cesarean delivery, the nurse notes she is drowsy, her respiratory rate has fallen to 9/min, and her patellar reflexes are absent. Urine output over the last hour was 12 mL. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?

choices.text: Continue the magnesium infusion at the current rate because absent reflexes are an expected therapeutic effect
choices.rationale: Loss of deep tendon reflexes together with respiratory depression signals magnesium toxicity, not a desired therapeutic endpoint, so continuing the infusion would worsen toxicity.

choices.text: Stop the magnesium infusion and administer intravenous calcium gluconate
choices.rationale: Areflexia and respiratory depression indicate serum magnesium has reached toxic levels, so the infusion must be stopped and calcium gluconate given to directly antagonize magnesium at the neuromuscular junction.

choices.text: Administer intravenous furosemide to increase renal magnesium clearance
choices.rationale: Although diuresis enhances magnesium excretion, in a patient with oliguria and impending respiratory failure it acts too slowly and risks hypovolemia, so it is not the immediate rescue.

choices.text: Increase the magnesium infusion rate to better control the blood pressure
choices.rationale: Magnesium is given for seizure prophylaxis rather than as an antihypertensive, and increasing the dose in a toxic patient would precipitate cardiac and respiratory arrest.

choices.text: Administer flumazenil to reverse the sedation
choices.rationale: The depressed sensorium is from hypermagnesemia, not benzodiazepines, so flumazenil has no role and would delay appropriate treatment.

explanation: Magnesium sulfate is the first-line agent for seizure prophylaxis and treatment in preeclampsia/eclampsia, but it has a narrow therapeutic window and is renally cleared, so oliguria predisposes to accumulation. Toxicity follows a predictable progression: loss of deep tendon reflexes at roughly 4–5 mmol/L, respiratory depression at about 5–7.5 mmol/L, and cardiac arrest at higher levels. When toxicity is recognized, stop the infusion immediately and give IV calcium gluconate, which antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction; support ventilation as needed. Reduced urine output should prompt closer monitoring and dose reduction because the kidney is the primary route of elimination.
subtopic: Hypertensive disorders — magnesium sulfate toxicity

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook magnesium toxicity presentation (drowsiness, RR 9/min, absent patellar reflexes, oliguria 12 mL/hr on a renally-cleared drug). Keyed answer (index 1: stop MgSO4 + IV calcium gluconate) is the single correct, guideline-concordant management — calcium antagonizes magnesium at the NMJ. All distractors are genuinely inferior: continuing the infusion (areflexia + RR 9 is toxicity, not a therapeutic endpoint), furosemide (too slow, risky with oliguria/hypovolemia), increasing the rate (Mg is not an antihypertensive and would precipitate arrest), and flumazenil (no benzodiazepine involved). The explanation's toxicity thresholds (loss of DTRs ~4-5 mmol/L, respiratory depression ~5-7.5 mmol/L, cardiac arrest higher) are accurate and current. REFERENCE: reference_url (StatPearls 'Magnesium Sulfate', NBK554553) resolves and is directly on-topic — it confirms the eclampsia/preeclampsia seizure-prophylaxis indication, the areflexia/respiratory-depression toxicity signs, calcium gluconate as treatment, and the exact serum thresholds cited (4-5 mmol/L for lost patellar reflexes, 5-7.5 mmol/L for respiratory depression). Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: A 38-year-old G3P2 woman at 38 weeks gestation with a BMI of 41 kg/m2 and poorly controlled gastroesophageal reflux requires emergent general anesthesia for a category-1 cesarean delivery after a prolonged fetal bradycardia; a functioning neuraxial block could not be established in time. She last ate 90 minutes ago. Compared with a non-pregnant patient of similar weight, which set of physiologic considerations BEST explains why this induction is especially high-risk and dictates a rapid-sequence approach with optimized preoxygenation?

choices.text: Increased functional residual capacity and decreased oxygen consumption, which delay desaturation and allow a relaxed, unhurried induction
choices.rationale: This reverses the true physiology; pregnancy reduces FRC and raises oxygen consumption, so it does not explain the heightened risk.

choices.text: Increased lower esophageal sphincter tone and accelerated gastric emptying, which lower aspiration risk and make cricoid pressure unnecessary
choices.rationale: Progesterone reduces lower esophageal sphincter tone and an emergent labor setting delays gastric emptying, so aspiration risk is increased, not decreased.

choices.text: An airway that is easier to intubate because of reduced mucosal vascularity and decreased Mallampati scores near term
choices.rationale: Pregnancy causes airway mucosal edema and vascular engorgement with higher Mallampati scores and a markedly increased failed-intubation rate, so the airway is harder, not easier.

choices.text: A higher MAC requirement for volatile agents and resistance to induction drugs, mandating larger doses
choices.rationale: Pregnancy actually decreases MAC and increases sensitivity to anesthetic and sedative agents, the opposite of what this option claims.

choices.text: Reduced functional residual capacity with increased oxygen consumption causing rapid desaturation, plus reduced lower esophageal sphincter tone and a vascular, edematous airway raising aspiration and failed-intubation risk
choices.rationale: This correctly combines the key changes—diminished oxygen reserve, heightened aspiration risk, and a difficult, friable airway—that together justify rapid-sequence induction with cricoid pressure and meticulous preoxygenation.

explanation: General anesthesia in the parturient is high-risk for two converging reasons. First, respiratory changes—reduced functional residual capacity from the gravid uterus plus increased oxygen consumption—cause rapid desaturation during apnea, so thorough preoxygenation/denitrogenation is essential. Second, aspiration risk is elevated by progesterone-mediated reduction in lower esophageal sphincter tone, mechanical displacement of the stomach, and delayed gastric emptying in labor, while the airway itself is more difficult because of mucosal edema and vascular engorgement (failed intubation is several-fold more common than in non-pregnant patients). These factors mandate a rapid-sequence induction with cricoid pressure, ready difficult-airway equipment, and left uterine displacement.
subtopic: Physiologic changes of pregnancy — aspiration and airway risk in general anesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 4) is the single best, comprehensive option, correctly combining reduced FRC + increased O2 consumption (rapid desaturation), reduced LES tone/delayed gastric emptying (aspiration risk), and a vascular/edematous airway (failed intubation risk). All four distractors contain unambiguous physiologic reversals and are genuinely inferior: choice 0 falsely claims increased FRC/decreased O2 consumption; choice 1 falsely claims increased LES tone/accelerated emptying; choice 2 falsely claims an easier airway; choice 3 falsely claims increased MAC/drug resistance (pregnancy decreases MAC ~30-40% and increases sensitivity). The explanation is accurate and current (RSI with cricoid pressure, left uterine displacement, several-fold higher failed-intubation rate). Cricoid pressure is debated in modern practice but remains the standard board answer, so no ambiguity. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia 'Airway Considerations in Pregnancy' URL resolves to genuine, on-topic content confirming FRC reduction of 10-25%, increased O2 consumption narrowing apnea margin, airway mucosal edema/vascularity worsening in labor, and RSI to reduce aspiration risk. Citation is valid and directly supports the question. Both checks pass.

stem: A 58-year-old man (BMI 41 kg/m^2) with witnessed apneas, loud snoring, and treated hypertension presents to a freestanding ambulatory surgery center for an elective open inguinal hernia repair under general anesthesia. He is not on home CPAP and has never had a sleep study. His STOP-BANG score is calculated at 6. The surgeon plans a same-day discharge, and the anticipated postoperative regimen includes scheduled oral oxycodone. Vitals are stable and the airway exam shows a Mallampati III. Which of the following is the most appropriate perioperative decision regarding this patient's care?

choices.text: Proceed at the freestanding center and discharge home the same day with the planned oxycodone regimen and routine instructions
choices.rationale: A high STOP-BANG score with presumed severe OSA plus anticipated postoperative opioids is an exclusion criterion for an isolated freestanding ASC, so routine same-day discharge with opioids is unsafe.

choices.text: Cancel surgery indefinitely until formal polysomnography and CPAP titration are completed
choices.rationale: Surgery need not be canceled solely for unconfirmed OSA; presumptive perioperative precautions and an appropriate care setting allow the case to proceed without mandatory preoperative polysomnography.

choices.text: Optimize with a multimodal opioid-sparing/regional plan and arrange the case where extended postoperative monitoring is available rather than routine freestanding same-day discharge
choices.rationale: SAMBA guidance treats a presumed-severe OSA patient requiring postoperative opioids as needing opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia and a setting with extended monitoring, making this the safest applied decision.

choices.text: Proceed with monitored anesthesia care and deep sedation instead of general anesthesia to avoid airway instrumentation
choices.rationale: Deep sedation without a secured airway can worsen upper-airway collapse in severe OSA and does not address the core issues of opioid use and disposition.

choices.text: Proceed as planned but substitute intramuscular meperidine for oral oxycodone to reduce respiratory depression risk
choices.rationale: Meperidine is a long-acting opioid with an active metabolite and offers no respiratory-safety advantage in OSA, so it does not resolve the disposition or opioid-sparing concern.

explanation: Patients with presumed or confirmed severe OSA who will require postoperative opioids fall outside the safe envelope for an isolated freestanding ambulatory center and routine same-day discharge. SAMBA recommendations emphasize identifying these patients (e.g., STOP-BANG >= 3, here 6), employing opioid-sparing multimodal and regional analgesia, continuing/initiating positive-airway-pressure therapy when indicated, and selecting a care setting that permits extended postoperative respiratory monitoring. Cancellation for unconfirmed OSA is not required when these mitigations are in place.
subtopic: Patient and procedure selection for ambulatory surgery (OSA screening and disposition)

verdict.reason: Clinical content is sound and the keyed answer (index 2) is the single best, unambiguous answer. Per SAMBA consensus guidance, a patient with presumed severe OSA (STOP-BANG 6, BMI 41, witnessed apneas) who will require scheduled postoperative opioids falls outside the safe envelope for an isolated freestanding ASC with routine same-day discharge; the correct management is opioid-sparing multimodal/regional analgesia plus a setting with extended postoperative respiratory monitoring. All distractors are genuinely inferior: routine same-day opioid discharge (0) is unsafe; indefinite cancellation pending PSG/CPAP titration (1) is not required for unconfirmed OSA; deep sedation/MAC without a secured airway (3) worsens upper-airway collapse and ignores opioid/disposition issues; IM meperidine (4) offers no respiratory advantage and has an active metabolite (normeperidine). One trivial imprecision: distractor 4's rationale calls meperidine 'long-acting' when it is actually relatively short-acting, but the operative point (active metabolite, no OSA respiratory benefit) is correct and this does not affect the key or create ambiguity. The reference URL (OpenAnesthesia 'Ambulatory Anesthesia') resolves, is current (updated 02/2026), and is directly on-topic, explicitly covering STOP-BANG OSA screening, ambulatory patient selection, and opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia.

stem: A healthy 34-year-old woman (70 kg) undergoes an ultrasound-guided interscalene brachial plexus block for outpatient shoulder arthroscopy at an ambulatory surgery center. Approximately 90 seconds after injection of 25 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine, she reports perioral numbness and a metallic taste, then becomes confused and has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Her heart rate falls to 38/min with a widening QRS on the monitor. Oxygen is applied and the airway is supported. Which of the following is the most appropriate next pharmacologic intervention?

choices.text: Administer 20% intravenous lipid emulsion as a 1.5 mL/kg bolus followed by an infusion
choices.rationale: An approximately 100 mL (1.5 mL/kg) bolus of 20% lipid emulsion with subsequent infusion is the definitive therapy for local anesthetic systemic toxicity and should be started as soon as LAST is recognized.

choices.text: Give a standard induction dose of propofol (2 mg/kg) to terminate the seizure
choices.rationale: A full induction dose of propofol can deepen cardiovascular depression in an already bradycardic, hemodynamically unstable patient and is not a substitute for lipid emulsion in LAST.

choices.text: Administer a high-dose epinephrine bolus of 1 mg IV per standard ACLS dosing
choices.rationale: In LAST, epinephrine should be used in reduced doses (<= 1 mcg/kg) because large boluses can impair resuscitation and provoke arrhythmias; a 1 mg bolus is inappropriate at this stage.

choices.text: Start an amiodarone infusion to treat the widening QRS complex
choices.rationale: Amiodarone and other sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmics are contraindicated in LAST because they compound the local anesthetic's sodium-channel blockade.

choices.text: Administer dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV
choices.rationale: Dantrolene treats malignant hyperthermia and has no role in local anesthetic systemic toxicity.

explanation: This is classic local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST): prodromal CNS symptoms followed rapidly by seizure and cardiovascular toxicity (bradycardia, QRS widening) after a large-volume long-acting local anesthetic injection. Per ASRA management, after airway and oxygenation support, 20% intravenous lipid emulsion (approximately 1.5 mL/kg bolus then infusion) is the cornerstone therapy and should be given early. Epinephrine should be reduced to <= 1 mcg/kg, and propofol, vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta blockers, and sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmics should be avoided.
subtopic: Regional anesthesia complications in the ambulatory setting (LAST management)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound. Classic LAST presentation (prodromal perioral numbness/metallic taste, seizure, then bradycardia + widening QRS) after intravascular injection of 25 mL 0.5% ropivacaine (125 mg) during interscalene block. The keyed answer — 20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg bolus (~100 mL) then infusion — is the unambiguous single best ASRA-recommended cornerstone therapy, given early once serious LAST is recognized. All distractors are genuinely inferior: full induction-dose propofol worsens cardiovascular depression; a 1 mg epinephrine ACLS bolus violates the ASRA ≤1 mcg/kg recommendation; dantrolene has no role (MH only). There is no competing defensible option (no benzodiazepine is offered, so lipid is clearly the next pharmacologic step). One minor imprecision: the amiodarone rationale states it is contraindicated because it 'compounds sodium-channel blockade,' but amiodarone is predominantly a class III (K-channel) agent and ASRA actually lists it as the preferred antiarrhythmic IF one is needed; class I sodium-channel blockers (lidocaine/procainamide) are the ones to avoid. This is a distractor-rationale wording slip only and does not alter the key or create ambiguity — amiodarone is still clearly inferior to lipid as the 'next' intervention. REFERENCE: WebFetched successfully; the OpenAnesthesia LAST page resolves, is directly on-topic, and corroborates the 1.5 mL/kg lipid bolus and the ≤1 mcg/kg reduced epinephrine dosing. Net: clinically correct, unambiguous, valid citation — keep.

stem: A 47-year-old woman is recovering in the Phase I PACU after an outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. Thirty minutes after arrival she is awake and follows commands fully, moves all four extremities on request, and maintains an SpO2 of 97% on room air. Her blood pressure is 128/78 mmHg (preoperative baseline 124/76 mmHg), and she is breathing deeply and coughing freely. Using the Modified Aldrete scoring system, what is this patient's score, and is she ready for Phase I discharge?

choices.text: Score 8 - not yet ready for Phase I discharge
choices.rationale: An 8 would require a 1-point deduction in one domain, but this patient meets full criteria in every Aldrete category, so 8 underestimates her score.

choices.text: Score 9 - ready for Phase I discharge
choices.rationale: A score of 9 implies a 1-point deduction somewhere, yet her oxygen saturation on room air, full consciousness, stable circulation, activity, and respiration each earn the maximum 2 points.

choices.text: Score 10 - ready for Phase I discharge
choices.rationale: She scores the full 2 points in all five domains (activity, respiration, circulation, consciousness, oxygenation) for a total of 10, which meets the threshold (>= 9) for Phase I discharge.

choices.text: Score 10 - but not ready because the Aldrete score also requires documented oral intake and voiding
choices.rationale: Oral intake and voiding are elements of the Phase II PADSS home-readiness assessment, not the Modified Aldrete score, so they are not required for Phase I discharge.

choices.text: Score 7 - not ready for Phase I discharge
choices.rationale: A 7 would require deductions totaling 3 points across domains, which does not match a patient meeting full criteria in every category.

explanation: The Modified Aldrete score evaluates five domains - activity, respiration, circulation, consciousness, and oxygen saturation - each scored 0 to 2, with a total of 9 or greater indicating readiness for Phase I PACU discharge. This patient earns full marks in every domain (moves four extremities, breathes deeply/coughs freely, BP within 20% of baseline, fully awake, SpO2 > 92% on room air) for a total of 10. Criteria such as tolerating oral intake and voiding belong to the Postanesthetic Discharge Scoring System (PADSS) used for Phase II home readiness, not the Aldrete score.
subtopic: PACU discharge readiness criteria (Modified Aldrete vs PADSS)
reference_title: Aldrete Scoring System - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 2, "Score 10 - ready for Phase I discharge") is the single best answer and is unambiguous. The Modified Aldrete score has 5 domains (activity, respiration, circulation, consciousness, SpO2), each 0-2, max 10. This patient earns 2 in every domain: moves all 4 extremities (2), breathes deeply/coughs (2), BP 128/78 within 20% of baseline 124/76 (2), fully awake (2), SpO2 97% on room air i.e. >=92% RA (2) = 10. All distractors are genuinely inferior: Score 8 and Score 7 require nonexistent deductions; Score 9 is wrong because no domain loses a point (its rationale correctly explains this); "Score 10 but needs oral intake/voiding" is a correctly-built trap since oral intake and voiding belong to PADSS/Phase II home-readiness, not the 5-domain Aldrete. Explanation and PADSS attribution are accurate.

One minor imprecision worth noting (does not affect the answer): the explanation states the discharge threshold is ">=9," while the cited StatPearls page states ">=8." This is a known literature discrepancy (original Aldrete/StatPearls use >=8; many texts cite >=9). It is non-material here because the patient scores a perfect 10 and clears either threshold; answer selection is unaffected.

REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK594237/ resolved successfully. Page title is "Aldrete Scoring System" (StatPearls). It accurately covers the original and modified Aldrete (5 domains, 0-2 each, max 10), pulse oximetry replacing color in 1995, and distinguishes Aldrete from PADSS. Citation is on-topic and matches the reference_title. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: An 84-year-old woman with hypertension and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is undergoing cemented hip hemiarthroplasty for a displaced femoral neck fracture under general anesthesia. She is hemodynamically stable with a blood pressure of 128/72 mmHg and SpO2 of 98% on FiO2 0.5. Within two minutes of the surgeon pressurizing methyl methacrylate cement into the femoral canal and impacting the prosthesis, her blood pressure falls to 64/38 mmHg, end-tidal CO2 drops from 34 to 19 mmHg, and SpO2 declines to 88%. The surgical field shows no obvious bleeding. Which of the following best explains this deterioration?

choices.text: Acute hemorrhagic shock from an unrecognized femoral arterial injury
choices.rationale: Major hemorrhage would produce hypotension but not the simultaneous abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2 with a dry surgical field, and the temporal link to cementation points elsewhere.

choices.text: Type I IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to the cephalosporin given at induction
choices.rationale: Anaphylaxis typically presents earlier after the antibiotic and is usually accompanied by bronchospasm, urticaria, or angioedema rather than being tightly time-locked to cement pressurization.

choices.text: Transfusion-related acute lung injury from intraoperative blood products
choices.rationale: TRALI causes hypoxemia and hypotension but requires a temporal relationship to a transfusion, which is not described, and does not explain the sudden capnographic drop at the moment of cementing.

choices.text: Bone cement implantation syndrome from methyl methacrylate monomer and marrow embolization
choices.rationale: The hypotension, hypoxemia, and abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2 occurring within minutes of cement pressurization and prosthesis impaction are the classic triad of bone cement implantation syndrome.

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity from absorption of wound-infiltrated bupivacaine
choices.rationale: LAST would more typically cause CNS excitation or arrhythmias and would not be expected to coincide precisely with cement insertion under general anesthesia.

explanation: Bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS) is characterized by hypoxemia, hypotension, and/or sudden loss of consciousness occurring around the time of cementation, prosthesis insertion, or joint reduction. The drop in end-tidal CO2 reflects an acute fall in pulmonary blood flow from embolization of marrow contents and methyl methacrylate monomer, with histamine and complement-mediated effects contributing to vasodilation and right heart strain. Elderly patients with cardiopulmonary disease undergoing cemented hemiarthroplasty for hip fracture are at highest risk, and management is supportive: increase FiO2 to 100%, give fluids and vasopressors, and maintain close communication with the surgeon.
subtopic: Orthopedics — bone cement implantation syndrome during cemented hemiarthroplasty
reference_title: Methyl Methacrylate Complications and Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome (OpenAnesthesia)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 3, bone cement implantation syndrome) is the single best answer and is unambiguous. The vignette is a textbook BCIS presentation — an 84-year-old with HTN/COPD undergoing CEMENTED hip hemiarthroplasty who develops the classic triad of hypotension, hypoxemia, and abrupt EtCO2 fall within 2 minutes of cement pressurization and prosthesis impaction. The EtCO2 drop correctly reflects acute reduction in pulmonary blood flow from marrow/monomer embolization and increased dead space from RV strain. All distractors are genuinely inferior: hemorrhage (A) is excluded by the dry field and tight temporal lock; cephalosporin anaphylaxis (B) would typically present earlier with cutaneous/bronchospastic signs; TRALI (C) requires an undescribed transfusion; LAST (E) from wound infiltration has slower absorption and a CNS/cardiac toxicity pattern, with timing coincidental. Risk factors (elderly, cardiopulmonary disease, cemented hemiarthroplasty for hip fracture) and management (FiO2 100%, fluids, vasopressors, surgeon communication) are accurate and current. The reference to histamine/complement-mediated vasodilation reflects commonly cited contributing theories and is not an error. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolved successfully and is squarely on-topic — it covers MMA complications and BCIS, including the hypotension/hypoxia/arrhythmia spectrum, RV failure from increased pulmonary artery pressure, decreased end-tidal CO2 as an embolization finding, HTN as a risk factor, and supportive management. Both dimensions sound.

stem: A 32-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury at the T4 level two years ago presents for cystoscopy and bladder stone removal. He declines neuraxial anesthesia and the case is started under monitored anesthesia care with light sedation. Shortly after the cystoscope is introduced and the bladder is distended with irrigation, his blood pressure rises abruptly from 112/70 to 212/118 mmHg, his heart rate falls to 46/min, and he reports a pounding headache with flushing and sweating noted above the clavicles. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.text: Place the patient flat in Trendelenburg and give a rapid intravenous fluid bolus
choices.rationale: Lying the patient flat would worsen the hypertensive crisis, since the correct positioning maneuver is to sit the patient upright to promote orthostatic blood pressure reduction.

choices.text: Stop the bladder distention and drain the bladder to remove the noxious stimulus
choices.rationale: Removing the inciting stimulus below the level of injury is the essential first step in autonomic dysreflexia, as it often resolves the crisis without further pharmacologic therapy.

choices.text: Administer atropine to treat the bradycardia before addressing the blood pressure
choices.rationale: The bradycardia is a baroreceptor-mediated reflex response to severe hypertension and will resolve once the blood pressure is controlled, so treating it first is misdirected.

choices.text: Give a beta-blocker such as esmolol as the sole agent to control the hypertension
choices.rationale: Unopposed beta-blockade can leave alpha-mediated vasoconstriction intact and is not the first maneuver; removing the stimulus and using a rapid-onset vasodilator are preferred.

choices.text: Deepen sedation with additional propofol and continue the procedure unchanged
choices.rationale: Deepening sedation does not interrupt the spinal reflex arc driving the crisis, and continuing the noxious stimulus allows the dangerous hypertension to persist.

explanation: Autonomic dysreflexia occurs in patients with spinal cord injury at or above T6 when a noxious stimulus below the level of injury (here, bladder distention) triggers unchecked sympathetic outflow, producing severe hypertension with reflex bradycardia and symptoms such as headache, flushing, and sweating above the lesion. The first and most effective step is to remove the inciting stimulus, sit the patient upright, and loosen constrictive items; if hypertension persists, a rapid-onset, short-acting agent such as nitroprusside, nitroglycerin, or nifedipine is used. Neuraxial anesthesia is protective because it blocks the afferent and efferent limbs of the reflex arc, which is why regional techniques are favored for these procedures.
subtopic: Urology — autonomic dysreflexia during cystoscopy in spinal cord injury

verdict.reason: Clinical: Classic autonomic dysreflexia in a T4 (i.e., at/above T6) complete SCI patient triggered by bladder distention during cystoscopy, with the hallmark triad of severe hypertension, reflex bradycardia, and above-lesion symptoms (headache, flushing, sweating above the clavicles). The keyed answer (index 1, drain the bladder/remove the noxious stimulus) is the single best immediate intervention and is the only option addressing the actual trigger; it frequently resolves the crisis alone. All four distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: Trendelenburg/fluid bolus worsens HTN (upright positioning is correct); atropine is misdirected because the bradycardia is baroreceptor-mediated and resolves with BP control; beta-blocker monotherapy risks unopposed alpha vasoconstriction; deepening sedation does not interrupt the spinal reflex arc. The explanation's pharmacology (rapid-onset nitroprusside/nitroglycerin/nifedipine if HTN persists) and the claim that neuraxial anesthesia is protective by blocking the reflex arc are accurate and current. No ambiguity among the choices since upright positioning is not offered as a standalone option. Reference: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482434/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Autonomic Dysreflexia' chapter, directly on-topic, confirming the at/above-T6 threshold, bladder distention as the most common trigger (~85%), upright positioning plus stimulus removal as first steps, and nitroglycerin/nifedipine as first-line agents.

stem: A 58-year-old woman with a BMI of 41 kg/m2 and hypertension undergoes a robotic-assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy that requires steep Trendelenburg positioning with both legs in stirrups for a total operative time of 5.5 hours. An epidural was placed for postoperative analgesia. Intraoperatively, mean arterial pressure was deliberately maintained around 60 mmHg. Approximately six hours after emergence, she reports increasingly severe, unrelenting pain in both calves that is markedly worsened by passive dorsiflexion of the toes, and the lower legs are tense and swollen. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Bilateral deep venous thrombosis of the calf veins
choices.rationale: Bilateral simultaneous DVT severe enough to cause this presentation is uncommon, and DVT does not characteristically produce the dramatic pain-on-passive-stretch that defines this clinical picture.

choices.text: Common peroneal nerve neuropraxia from stirrup compression
choices.rationale: Peroneal nerve injury causes foot drop and lateral leg sensory loss but not tense, painful, swollen compartments with pain on passive stretch, so it does not fit the full presentation.

choices.text: Well-leg compartment syndrome of the lower extremities
choices.rationale: Prolonged lithotomy with steep Trendelenburg, obesity, long operative time, and deliberate hypotension are classic risk factors, and the unrelenting pain worsened by passive stretch with tense swollen compartments is the hallmark of compartment syndrome.

choices.text: Epidural-related transient neurologic deficit from the analgesic infusion
choices.rationale: An epidural would be expected to mask rather than produce this severe localized calf pain, and a local-anesthetic effect would not create tense, swollen compartments.

choices.text: Bilateral femoral nerve injury from hip hyperflexion in stirrups
choices.rationale: Femoral nerve injury causes quadriceps weakness and anterior thigh sensory changes rather than the painful tense calf compartments described here.

explanation: Well-leg compartment syndrome is a feared complication of prolonged surgery in the lithotomy position, with risk compounded by steep Trendelenburg, elevated intra-abdominal pressure from pneumoperitoneum, obesity, long operative duration, and intraoperative hypotension that reduces lower-limb perfusion. Lowering the legs after a prolonged elevated period causes reperfusion of ischemic muscle, edema, and a rising intracompartmental pressure that can lead to muscle necrosis, foot drop, amputation, or death if not promptly decompressed. The cardinal feature is pain out of proportion that is worsened by passive stretch, and the most important interventions are early recognition, removing constrictive dressings, and urgent fasciotomy; periodic intraoperative lowering of the legs is a key preventive strategy.
subtopic: GYN/Urology — well-leg compartment syndrome from prolonged lithotomy positioning
reference_title: Compartment Syndrome Due to Patient Positioning (Compartment Syndrome, NCBI Bookshelf)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 2, well-leg compartment syndrome) is the single best answer. The vignette stacks the classic, well-documented risk factors for lithotomy-related lower-extremity compartment syndrome: prolonged lithotomy with stirrups, steep Trendelenburg, BMI 41 obesity, 5.5-hour operative time, and deliberate intraoperative hypotension (MAP ~60) reducing limb perfusion. The hallmark finding — severe unrelenting pain out of proportion, worsened by passive stretch (passive dorsiflexion of the toes stretches the deep posterior/calf compartment muscles), with tense, swollen compartments — is correctly described. Bilateral involvement is consistent with both legs in stirrups and actually supports WLCS over the nerve-injury distractors. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and the rationales are accurate: bilateral simultaneous DVT is uncommon and does not produce pain-on-passive-stretch/tense compartments; common peroneal neuropraxia causes foot drop/lateral sensory loss without tense compartments; an epidural would mask rather than cause this pain (and pain is present here, so masking is not the issue); femoral nerve injury affects quadriceps/anterior thigh, not calf compartments. The explanation (reperfusion edema after lowering legs, complications of necrosis/foot drop/amputation/death, urgent fasciotomy and removal of constrictive dressings as treatment, periodic intraoperative leg lowering as prevention) is medically accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553906/ resolves to the NCBI Bookshelf StatPearls chapter 'Compartment Syndrome Due to Patient Positioning,' which is directly on-topic — it emphasizes the lithotomy position, obesity, Trendelenburg, and operative time >5 hours as risk factors and fasciotomy as treatment. Citation is valid and matches the question. No clinical or reference problem.

stem: A 78-kg, 24-year-old woman undergoes ultrasound-guided interscalene brachial plexus block with 30 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine for shoulder arthroscopy. Roughly 90 seconds after injection, she becomes agitated, complains of perioral numbness and tinnitus, then has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Her rhythm degenerates into a wide-complex tachycardia and she loses her pulse. ACLS chest compressions are started and the airway is secured. In addition to high-quality CPR, which intervention is the most important next pharmacologic step?

choices.text: Administer 20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus, then begin an infusion at 0.25 mL/kg/min
choices.rationale: Correct: in local anesthetic systemic toxicity with cardiac arrest, an immediate 20% lipid emulsion bolus followed by infusion is the priority therapy that 'lipid-sinks' the drug and supports the failing myocardium.

choices.text: Give a 1 mg IV bolus of epinephrine per the standard ACLS dose and repeat every 3-5 minutes
choices.rationale: Incorrect: in LAST, large standard epinephrine boluses can worsen arrhythmias and impair resuscitation, so reduced doses (≤1 mcg/kg) are recommended rather than the full 1 mg ACLS dose.

choices.text: Administer vasopressin 40 units IV as the first vasopressor
choices.rationale: Incorrect: vasopressin is specifically avoided in LAST resuscitation because it is associated with worse outcomes such as pulmonary edema in experimental models.

choices.text: Give lidocaine 1.5 mg/kg IV to treat the ventricular arrhythmia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: lidocaine is itself a sodium-channel-blocking local anesthetic and would add to the toxic burden, so it is contraindicated for arrhythmias caused by LAST.

choices.text: Administer flumazenil to reverse the central nervous system depression
choices.rationale: Incorrect: flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not local anesthetics, and would lower the seizure threshold rather than treat local anesthetic toxicity.

explanation: Prodromal CNS symptoms (perioral numbness, tinnitus, agitation) followed by seizure and cardiovascular collapse after a high-volume long-acting local anesthetic injection is the classic picture of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST). Management modifies ACLS: reduce epinephrine to ≤1 mcg/kg boluses, avoid vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta blockers, and additional local anesthetics such as lidocaine, and give 20% lipid emulsion immediately (1.5 mL/kg bolus, then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion). Propofol is not a substitute for lipid emulsion in arrest because its lipid content is too low and it is a myocardial depressant.
subtopic: Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST) Management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous.
